guest_role: CPO and CTO at Deliveroo, CPO at Monzo, co-founder of Sling
assertiveness_why: Harry asks prepared, multi-sentence questions and follows up well, but remains largely receptive—exploring Mike's thinking without contesting claims or planting his own thesis.
question_style: Harry asks thoughtful follow-ups about product decisions, strategy, and philosophy. He's curious and engaged but doesn't challenge positions; mostly seeks deeper understanding. Includes a quick-fire round at the end.
tone: Warm and collegial. Harry mentions meeting Mike years ago and wanting to do this interview for a long time. Long-form, conversational, respectful throughout.
depth: Very deep. Covers team structure, product simplicity vs features, data interpretation and misuse, A/B testing, team morale, regulatory constraints, founder mode, competition strategy, launch psychology, and work-life integration. Meanders through multiple companies and philosophies.
harry_quotes: How do you feel about founder mode?
What was the best product decision you made at Facebook?
learnings: Product excellence means helping users achieve goals with minimal effort; optimal teams are 6-8 people—mostly engineers, plus one data scientist and designer, PM optional.
Develop a theory of the world before building; most theories are partially wrong, but shipping reveals where; the hard part is iterating through misunderstanding rather than giving up early.
Feature bloat has hidden costs (maintenance, explanation, opportunity cost); 'nice to have' features almost always cost more to build and return less value than expected.
Product is art guided by science; intuition about user psychology must be grounded in quantitative experiments and metrics, not pure data or pure instinct.

Real-time logistics (food delivery) creates urgency and rapid iteration culture different from social media; brand promise (32-minute delivery) requires infrastructure discipline that changes product thinking.
Regulatory constraints change incentive structures; being a bank shifted thinking from 'maximize engagement' to 'build trust and stability as societal goods,' which enabled different product bets.
Fewer, more targeted product bets often outperform competitors shipping faster; conviction in the theory of the world matters more than matching feature velocity.
notable: Mike discusses obsessive behavior (checking a transaction Slack channel every 3-4 minutes) and the importance of distinguishing between healthy 'hard work' and unhealthy 'obsessive theater'; also extensively addresses balancing fatherhood with startup intensity, including gardening as a mental reset.
